nativeRange.habitat: Steep current-swept outer reef drop-offs at depths of roughly 25–100 m, typically schooling well above the substrate in open water

diet.notes: Genicanthus is the only zooplanktivorous genus within the angelfish family Pomacanthidae. In the wild has been documented forming midwater shoals to feed on plankton above current-swept reef drop-offs, with some incidental consumption of small benthic invertebrates (polychaetes, bryozoans) and algae. In aquariums, generally advised to offer a varied diet of vitamin-enriched mysis and frozen brine shrimp, finely-chopped meaty foods, Spirulina, and marine algae preparations, fed several small meals per day.

summary: The Bellus Angelfish is a small, peaceful, sexually dimorphic planktivore from deep Indo-Pacific reef slopes, generally considered reef-safe and commonly advised for larger reef aquariums with strong current and open swimming space.
careNotes: Genicanthus bellus belongs to the only planktivorous genus within the angelfish family Pomacanthidae and is generally considered safer around corals and sessile invertebrates than most other marine angelfishes. The species is strongly sexually dimorphic: males display an iridescent pale white-to-blue body with long horizontal blue stripes and a distinctive yellow-to-orange stripe on the lower body and anal fin, while females are pale blue-grey with a striking black-edged yellow/orange band running from the gills through the dorsal fin and along the lateral line to the tail. In the wild it tends to form harems of roughly 3–7 individuals (one dominant male with several females) on outer reef drop-offs in 25–100 m of water, where it shoals in midwater to feed on plankton. Only one male should be kept per aquarium — males have been known to fight, sometimes to the death, with each other and with closely related Genicanthus species. A male-with-several-females harem or a single male-female pair is the recommended configuration. Because it is a deep-water, current-loving, midwater swimmer, the species generally requires ample open swimming space above the rockwork and a tank with a long footprint and strong flow. A dimly-lit acclimation environment is commonly advised to reduce shipping stress. Temperature spread noted between sources: LiveAquaria cites 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), Fishkeeping World cites 72–76°F (22–24°C), and FishBase's preferred range is 21.5–28.9°C with a mean around 26.1°C; the cross-source averaged range of roughly 22–26°C is used here.
breedingNotes: Genicanthus bellus is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite: all newly mature fish are female, and in the absence of a male the dominant female can transition into a male in as little as a fortnight by upregulating testosterone production. Wild groups follow a harem/lek-style mating system with dominant males controlling territory along the drop-off. Captive breeding has been reported by experienced aquarists but remains uncommon; pelagic eggs and larvae are difficult to rear.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (18.0 cm TL), preferred temperature (21.5–28.9°C, mean 26.1°C), depth (24–110 m, usually 50–100 m), native range, taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), trophic level (3.4 ± 0.45, planktivorous), and harem size (3–7 individuals). FishBase's upper temperature ceiling (28.9°C) is notably warmer than LiveAquaria's (25.6°C); the file uses the averaged 22–26°C range.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Genicanthus bellus)
additional.notes: Confirmed max length 18 cm, distribution across western Pacific and eastern Indian Ocean, depth 25–100 m, protogynous hermaphroditism with female-to-male transition in ~two weeks, harem structure of 3–7 fish with lekking-style mating, planktivorous diet supplemented by small benthic invertebrates and algae.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank-size guidance (~120 gal / 454 L for groups, with 70 gal cited for a single fish), temperature 72–76°F (22–24°C), and confirmed adult size around 7 inches (18 cm). Tank guidance averaged with LiveAquaria's 120 gal and rounded up to 500 L given midwater/current-swept lifestyle and haremic grouping.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite with harem/lek mating; captive spawning reported but uncommon and pelagic eggs/larvae are difficult to rear.
